content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:031748ae_abs

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript is force-ingested and contains durable structured facts about the user's location, job role, team size, leadership concerns, and outing preferences/plans, plus richer context about delegation and team-bonding rationale.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:031748ae_abs:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user has a new role as Senior Software Engineer.
- The user leads their engineering team in their new role as Senior Software Engineer.
- The user planned for 5 people from their team to attend the outing: 4 engineers plus manager Rachel.
- The user is planning a team outing for their engineering team with fun activities in the city.
- The user selected City View Rooftop as a promising option for the team outing venue.
- The user planned to ask Samantha about the Corporate Team Building Package for 6 people at City View Rooftop.
- The user intended to consider structured activities for the team outing to encourage teamwork and collaboration.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-8: responsible_for; The user leads their engineering team in their new role as Senior Software Engineer.; scope=Applies to the user's new Senior Software Engineer role as stated in the 2023-05-23 session.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-10: works_on_project; The user has a new role as Senior Software Engineer.; scope=Applies to the user's work role as stated in the 2023-05-23 session.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-9: current_metric_value; The user's engineering team has 4 engineers.; scope=Applies to the user's engineering team as of the 2023-05-23 session.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-4: has_goal; The user is planning a team outing for their engineering team with fun activities in the city.; scope=Applies to the 2023-05-23 team outing planning conversation.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-12: has_goal; The user planned for 5 people from their team to attend the outing: 4 engineers plus manager Rachel.; scope=Applies to the 2023-05-23 team outing attendance plan.
- note hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-note-2: PLAN; Team outing plan: City View Rooftop Corporate Team Building Package; The user was planning a team outing at City View Rooftop and intended to contact Samantha about the Corporate Team Building Package for 5 total attendees: 4 engineers and manager Rachel. The outing direction favored rooftop outdoor games such as Giant Jenga, with possible structured activities to encourage teamwork and collaboration.; scope=User's team outing planning context from LongMemEval 031748ae_abs session 1
- note hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-note-3: DIRECTION; Preferred format guidance for small engineering team outing; For the planned rooftop outing, the reusable format recommendation was a flexible 2–3 hour event with a light structure: some teamwork-oriented activities or icebreakers balanced with free mingling, snacks/drinks, and outdoor games. This approach was suggested to encourage bonding without making the event feel over-scheduled.; scope=Planning guidance for the user's small engineering team outing
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-11: responsible_for; Rachel is the user's manager.; scope=Applies to Rachel's relationship to the user as stated in the 2023-05-23 session.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-5: prefers; The user selected City View Rooftop as a promising option for the team outing venue.; scope=Applies to venue choice in the 2023-05-23 team outing planning conversation.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-7: has_goal; The user intended to consider structured activities for the team outing to encourage teamwork and collaboration.; scope=Applies to the 2023-05-23 team outing format planning conversation.
- claim hot-path-ac5f19c99b65-claim-6: has_goal; The user planned to ask Samantha about the Corporate Team Building Package for 6 people at City View Rooftop.; scope=Applies to the 2023-05-23 City View Rooftop outing planning conversation.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my / I'm; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I / my / I'm; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.
- mention=Senior Software Engineer; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<1>; type=existing; name=Senior Software Engineer; reason=Existing concept exactly matches the role mentioned in the transcript.
- mention=team / my team / our team; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<2>; type=existing; name=team / my team / our team; reason=Transcript and existing profile indicate this refers to the user's engineering team.

- mention=Mount Tamalpais State Park; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=LOCATION; name=Mount Tamalpais State Park; reason=Concrete park selected for the team hike; no existing matching candidate.
- mention=Rachel; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PERSON; name=Rachel; reason=Relevant context states Rachel is the user's manager and part of the team outing plan; no existing Rachel entity is listed.

- mention=Samantha; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PERSON; name=Samantha; reason=Relevant context says the user planned to ask Samantha about a team building package; no existing Samantha entity is listed.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/24 (Wed) 00:36
Transcript:
user: I'm planning a team outing and need some recommendations for a fun, outdoor activity that can accommodate 6 people.
assistant: What a great idea! Outdoor activities are an excellent way to bond with your team while getting some fresh air and exercise. Here are some fun outdoor activities that can accommodate 6 people:

1. **Escape Room Outdoor Adventure**: Many escape room companies offer outdoor adventures that involve solving puzzles and challenges while exploring the outdoors. This activity promotes teamwork, problem-solving, and communication.
2. **Group Hike**: Choose a nearby park or nature reserve and plan a guided hike. This activity encourages camaraderie, exercise, and appreciation for nature. You can also make it more interesting by adding a scavenger hunt or nature-themed challenges.
3. **Kayaking or Paddleboarding**: Rent kayaks or paddleboards and explore a nearby lake, river, or coastal area. This activity promotes teamwork, balance, and laughter.
4. **Outdoor Games**: Organize a day of outdoor games like capture the flag, frisbee golf, or bocce ball. You can also set up a badminton or volleyball net for a fun, casual game.
5. **Geocaching**: Create a treasure hunt using GPS coordinates, where teams have to find hidden containers called "geocaches." This activity encourages teamwork, problem-solving, and exploration.
6. **Bike Ride or Bike Tour**: Rent bikes and explore a local bike trail or take a guided bike tour. This activity promotes exercise, teamwork, and sightseeing.
7. **Outdoor Scavenger Hunt**: Create a list of items or challenges that teams need to complete around the city or a designated area. This activity encourages teamwork, problem-solving, and creativity.
8. **Picnic and Outdoor Games**: Plan a picnic and set up a few outdoor games like cornhole, ladder toss, or giant Jenga. This activity promotes socializing, relaxation, and fun.
9. **Ziplining or Tree Climbing**: For a more adventurous team, consider ziplining or tree climbing activities. These activities promote trust, teamwork, and adrenaline rushes.
10. **Outdoor Cooking or BBQ**: Plan a outdoor cooking or BBQ activity where teams can work together to prepare a meal or snacks. This activity promotes teamwork, communication, and culinary skills.

Remember to consider your team's interests, fitness levels, and abilities when choosing an activity. Make sure to also plan for safety, logistics, and any necessary equipment or permits.
user: That's a great list! I think I'll go with the group hike option. Do you know if there are any nearby parks or nature reserves that are suitable for a 6-person group? And by the way, I've been enjoying my role as Senior Software Engineer for a while, especially the part where I now lead a team of five engineers - it's been a great experience so far, and I'm excited to see how they'll grow under my guidance.
assistant: Congratulations on your role as Senior Software Engineer! It's great to hear that you're enjoying leading your team and excited to see them grow. That's fantastic!

Regarding the group hike, I'd be happy to help you find a suitable park or nature reserve. However, I'm a large language model, I don't have access to your current location. Could you please share your city or region with me? That way, I can provide you with some recommendations for nearby parks or nature reserves that would be suitable for a 6-person group.

Additionally, you might want to consider the following factors when choosing a park or nature reserve:

1. Distance and travel time: How far are you willing to travel for the hike?
2. Trail difficulty: Are you looking for a leisurely stroll or a more challenging hike?
3. Amenities: Do you need restrooms, parking, or picnic areas?
4. Scenic views: Are you looking for scenic views, waterfalls, or unique geological formations?

Once I know your location, I can provide you with some personalized recommendations for a great group hike!
user: I'm based in the San Francisco Bay Area. I'd prefer a park or nature reserve within a 30-45 minute drive from the city center. We're a relatively active group, so a moderate trail with some scenic views would be perfect.
assistant: The San Francisco Bay Area has plenty of beautiful parks and nature reserves that fit your criteria. Here are some recommendations within a 30-45 minute drive from the city center:

1. **Mount Tamalpais State Park** (30-40 minutes from SF): This park offers stunning views of the Bay Area, the Pacific Ocean, and the surrounding hills. The Steep Ravine Trail is a moderate 3.5-mile hike with scenic views of the ocean and waterfalls.
2. **Muir Woods National Monument** (35-45 minutes from SF): While you might think of Muir Woods as a touristy spot, it's still a beautiful place for a hike. The Dipsea Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through the redwoods and offers stunning views of the Pacific Ocean.
3. **Purisima Creek Redwoods Open Space Preserve** (30-40 minutes from SF): This preserve has over 24 miles of trails, but the Whittemore Gulch Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a lush forest and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.
4. **Wunderlich County Park** (30-40 minutes from SF): This park has several trails, but the Bear Gulch Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a forest of redwoods and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.
5. **Edgewood Park and Natural Preserve** (30-40 minutes from SF): This park has several trails, but the Edgewood Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a serpentine grassland and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.
6. **Pulgas Ridge Open Space Preserve** (30-40 minutes from SF): This preserve has several trails, but the Cordilleras Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a forest of redwoods and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.

All of these options offer moderate trails with scenic views, and they're within your desired driving distance from the city center. Be sure to check the park's website for up-to-date trail information, parking, and any specific regulations before you head out.

Which one of these options sounds appealing to you?
user: Mount Tamalpais State Park sounds great. I'll check the trail conditions and plan the hike accordingly. By the way, I've been thinking about our team's workload and I think I need to delegate some tasks more effectively to ensure we're meeting our project deadlines. Do you have any suggestions on how I can do that better?
assistant: Mount Tamalpais State Park is a fantastic choice.

Now, about delegating tasks effectively, I'm happy to help! As a senior software engineer and team lead, delegating tasks is a crucial skill to master. Here are some suggestions to help you delegate tasks more effectively:

1. **Clearly define tasks and objectives**: Break down larger tasks into smaller, manageable chunks, and ensure each task has a clear objective, scope, and deadline. This will help team members understand what's expected of them.
2. **Identify team members' strengths and weaknesses**: Get to know your team members' skills, expertise, and interests. This will help you delegate tasks that play to their strengths and provide opportunities for growth.
3. **Communicate effectively**: Clearly explain the task, its importance, and how it fits into the larger project scope. Be available to answer questions and provide guidance as needed.
4. **Set realistic deadlines and expectations**: Ensure the deadlines and expectations are realistic, considering the task's complexity, team member's workload, and potential roadblocks.
5. **Provide necessary resources and support**: Ensure team members have the necessary tools, training, and support to complete the task successfully.
6. **Establish a task tracking system**: Use a tool like Jira, Trello, or Asana to track tasks, progress, and deadlines. This will help you stay organized and keep the team informed.
7. **Regular check-ins and feedback**: Schedule regular check-ins to monitor progress, provide feedback, and address any challenges or concerns.
8. **Empower team members to take ownership**: Give team members the autonomy to make decisions and take ownership of their tasks. This will help them feel more invested in the project's success.
9. **Lead by example**: Demonstrate a willingness to roll up your sleeves and help with tasks when needed. This will show your team that you're committed to the project's success and willing to support them.
10. **Review and adjust**: Regularly review your delegation strategy and make adjustments as needed. Identify what's working and what areas need improvement.

By following these tips, you'll be able to delegate tasks more effectively, free up your time, and help your team grow and develop their skills.

Remember, delegating tasks is an ongoing process that requires communication, trust, and adaptability. With practice, you'll become more comfortable delegating tasks and seeing your team thrive.
user: That's really helpful, thank you! I think I need to work on setting realistic deadlines and expectations, as well as providing necessary resources and support to my team members. I'll make sure to implement these suggestions and see how it goes. By the way, I'm looking forward to our team hike at Mount Tamalpais State Park, it'll be a great opportunity to bond with my team and get some exercise. I'm really proud of the progress we've made so far and we're all working well together.
assistant: It's great to hear that you're proud of your team's progress! Leading a team of 5 engineers can be a challenging but rewarding experience. It's fantastic that you're recognizing the importance of team bonding and taking proactive steps to foster a positive team dynamic.

Setting realistic deadlines and expectations, as well as providing necessary resources and support, will go a long way in helping your team members feel empowered and motivated. Remember to also celebrate your team's successes and recognize individual contributions to keep morale high.

Now, I'm excited for your team hike at Mount Tamalpais State Park! It's a beautiful location, and I'm sure you'll all have a fantastic time exploring the trails and enjoying the scenic views. Don't forget to take plenty of photos and make some unforgettable memories with your team.

As a friendly reminder, consider incorporating some team-building activities or games during the hike to encourage collaboration and camaraderie. You could plan a scavenger hunt, play "Would You Rather," or even have a friendly competition to see who can spot the most interesting plants or animals. Whatever you choose, I'm sure it'll be a blast!

Have a fantastic time on your team hike, and I look forward to hearing about your experience!
user: I think I'll plan a scavenger hunt to keep things engaging and fun for the team. That way, we can all work together, enjoy the scenery, and have some friendly competition. Thanks for the suggestion!
assistant: A scavenger hunt is a perfect way to add an extra layer of excitement to your team hike! It's a great way to encourage teamwork, problem-solving, and communication while enjoying the beautiful scenery of Mount Tamalpais State Park.

Remember to create a list of items or challenges that are fun and achievable, and make sure to provide clear instructions and rules for the scavenger hunt. You could include items like:

* A specific type of leaf or flower
* A certain type of bird or insect
* A unique rock or mineral formation
* A scenic viewpoint or landmark
* A team photo in a specific location

You could also consider dividing the team into smaller groups to make it more competitive and exciting. Just make sure to set a time limit and a meeting point for everyone to gather and share their finds.

I'm sure your team will have an amazing time on the hike, and the scavenger hunt will add an extra layer of fun and engagement. Don't forget to take plenty of photos and share them with the team afterwards!

Have a fantastic time on your team hike, and I hope the scavenger hunt is a huge success!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm planning a team outing and need some recommendations for a fun, outdoor activity that can accommodate 6 people.
assistant: What a great idea! Outdoor activities are an excellent way to bond with your team while getting some fresh air and exercise. Here are some fun outdoor activities that can accommodate 6 people:

1. **Escape Room Outdoor Adventure**: Many escape room companies offer outdoor adventures that involve solving puzzles and challenges while exploring the outdoors. This activity promotes teamwork, problem-solving, and communication.
2. **Group Hike**: Choose a nearby park or nature reserve and plan a guided hike. This activity encourages camaraderie, exercise, and appreciation for nature. You can also make it more interesting by adding a scavenger hunt or nature-themed challenges.
3. **Kayaking or Paddleboarding**: Rent kayaks or paddleboards and explore a nearby lake, river, or coastal area. This activity promotes teamwork, balance, and laughter.
4. **Outdoor Games**: Organize a day of outdoor games like capture the flag, frisbee golf, or bocce ball. You can also set up a badminton or volleyball net for a fun, casual game.
5. **Geocaching**: Create a treasure hunt using GPS coordinates, where teams have to find hidden containers called "geocaches." This activity encourages teamwork, problem-solving, and exploration.
6. **Bike Ride or Bike Tour**: Rent bikes and explore a local bike trail or take a guided bike tour. This activity promotes exercise, teamwork, and sightseeing.
7. **Outdoor Scavenger Hunt**: Create a list of items or challenges that teams need to complete around the city or a designated area. This activity encourages teamwork, problem-solving, and creativity.
8. **Picnic and Outdoor Games**: Plan a picnic and set up a few outdoor games like cornhole, ladder toss, or giant Jenga. This activity promotes socializing, relaxation, and fun.
9. **Ziplining or Tree Climbing**: For a more adventurous team, consider ziplining or tree climbing activities. These activities promote trust, teamwork, and adrenaline rushes.
10. **Outdoor Cooking or BBQ**: Plan a outdoor cooking or BBQ activity where teams can work together to prepare a meal or snacks. This activity promotes teamwork, communication, and culinary skills.

Remember to consider your team's interests, fitness levels, and abilities when choosing an activity. Make sure to also plan for safety, logistics, and any necessary equipment or permits.
user: That's a great list! I think I'll go with the group hike option. Do you know if there are any nearby parks or nature reserves that are suitable for a 6-person group? And by the way, I've been enjoying my role as Senior Software Engineer for a while, especially the part where I now lead a team of five engineers - it's been a great experience so far, and I'm excited to see how they'll grow under my guidance.
assistant: Congratulations on your role as Senior Software Engineer! It's great to hear that you're enjoying leading your team and excited to see them grow. That's fantastic!

Regarding the group hike, I'd be happy to help you find a suitable park or nature reserve. However, I'm a large language model, I don't have access to your current location. Could you please share your city or region with me? That way, I can provide you with some recommendations for nearby parks or nature reserves that would be suitable for a 6-person group.

Additionally, you might want to consider the following factors when choosing a park or nature reserve:

1. Distance and travel time: How far are you willing to travel for the hike?
2. Trail difficulty: Are you looking for a leisurely stroll or a more challenging hike?
3. Amenities: Do you need restrooms, parking, or picnic areas?
4. Scenic views: Are you looking for scenic views, waterfalls, or unique geological formations?

Once I know your location, I can provide you with some personalized recommendations for a great group hike!
user: I'm based in the San Francisco Bay Area. I'd prefer a park or nature reserve within a 30-45 minute drive from the city center. We're a relatively active group, so a moderate trail with some scenic views would be perfect.
assistant: The San Francisco Bay Area has plenty of beautiful parks and nature reserves that fit your criteria. Here are some recommendations within a 30-45 minute drive from the city center:

1. **Mount Tamalpais State Park** (30-40 minutes from SF): This park offers stunning views of the Bay Area, the Pacific Ocean, and the surrounding hills. The Steep Ravine Trail is a moderate 3.5-mile hike with scenic views of the ocean and waterfalls.
2. **Muir Woods National Monument** (35-45 minutes from SF): While you might think of Muir Woods as a touristy spot, it's still a beautiful place for a hike. The Dipsea Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through the redwoods and offers stunning views of the Pacific Ocean.
3. **Purisima Creek Redwoods Open Space Preserve** (30-40 minutes from SF): This preserve has over 24 miles of trails, but the Whittemore Gulch Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a lush forest and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.
4. **Wunderlich County Park** (30-40 minutes from SF): This park has several trails, but the Bear Gulch Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a forest of redwoods and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.
5. **Edgewood Park and Natural Preserve** (30-40 minutes from SF): This park has several trails, but the Edgewood Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a serpentine grassland and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.
6. **Pulgas Ridge Open Space Preserve** (30-40 minutes from SF): This preserve has several trails, but the Cordilleras Trail is a moderate 4.5-mile hike that takes you through a forest of redwoods and offers scenic views of the surrounding hills.

All of these options offer moderate trails with scenic views, and they're within your desired driving distance from the city center. Be sure to check the park's website for up-to-date trail information, parking, and any specific regulations before you head out.

Which one of these options sounds appealing to you?
user: Mount Tamalpais State Park sounds great. I'll check the trail conditions and plan the hike accordingly. By the way, I've been thinking about our team's workload and I think I need to delegate some tasks more effectively to ensure we're meeting our project deadlines. Do you have any suggestions on how I can do that better?
assistant: Mount Tamalpais State Park is a fantastic choice.

Now, about delegating tasks effectively, I'm happy to help! As a senior software engineer and team lead, delegating tasks is a crucial skill to master. Here are some suggestions to help you delegate tasks more effectively:

1. **Clearly define tasks and objectives**: Break down larger tasks into smaller, manageable chunks, and ensure each task has a clear objective, scope, and deadline. This will help team members understand what's expected of them.
2. **Identify team members' strengths and weaknesses**: Get to know your team members' skills, expertise, and interests. This will help you delegate tasks that play to their strengths and provide opportunities for growth.
3. **Communicate effectively**: Clearly explain the task, its importance, and how it fits into the larger project scope. Be available to answer questions and provide guidance as needed.
4. **Set realistic deadlines and expectations**: Ensure the deadlines and expectations are realistic, considering the task's complexity, team member's workload, and potential roadblocks.
5. **Provide necessary resources and support**: Ensure team members have the necessary tools, training, and support to complete the task successfully.
6. **Establish a task tracking system**: Use a tool like Jira, Trello, or Asana to track tasks, progress, and deadlines. This will help you stay organized and keep the team informed.
7. **Regular check-ins and feedback**: Schedule regular check-ins to monitor progress, provide feedback, and address any challenges or concerns.
8. **Empower team members to take ownership**: Give team members the autonomy to make decisions and take ownership of their tasks. This will help them feel more invested in the project's success.
9. **Lead by example**: Demonstrate a willingness to roll up your sleeves and help with tasks when needed. This will show your team that you're committed to the project's success and willing to support them.
10. **Review and adjust**: Regularly review your delegation strategy and make adjustments as needed. Identify what's working and what areas need improvement.

By following these tips, you'll be able to delegate tasks more effectively, free up your time, and help your team grow and develop their skills.

Remember, delegating tasks is an ongoing process that requires communication, trust, and adaptability. With practice, you'll become more comfortable delegating tasks and seeing your team thrive.
user: That's really helpful, thank you! I think I need to work on setting realistic deadlines and expectations, as well as providing necessary resources and support to my team members. I'll make sure to implement these suggestions and see how it goes. By the way, I'm looking forward to our team hike at Mount Tamalpais State Park, it'll be a great opportunity to bond with my team and get some exercise. I'm really proud of the progress we've made so far and we're all working well together.
assistant: It's great to hear that you're proud of your team's progress! Leading a team of 5 engineers can be a challenging but rewarding experience. It's fantastic that you're recognizing the importance of team bonding and taking proactive steps to foster a positive team dynamic.

Setting realistic deadlines and expectations, as well as providing necessary resources and support, will go a long way in helping your team members feel empowered and motivated. Remember to also celebrate your team's successes and recognize individual contributions to keep morale high.

Now, I'm excited for your team hike at Mount Tamalpais State Park! It's a beautiful location, and I'm sure you'll all have a fantastic time exploring the trails and enjoying the scenic views. Don't forget to take plenty of photos and make some unforgettable memories with your team.

As a friendly reminder, consider incorporating some team-building activities or games during the hike to encourage collaboration and camaraderie. You could plan a scavenger hunt, play "Would You Rather," or even have a friendly competition to see who can spot the most interesting plants or animals. Whatever you choose, I'm sure it'll be a blast!

Have a fantastic time on your team hike, and I look forward to hearing about your experience!
user: I think I'll plan a scavenger hunt to keep things engaging and fun for the team. That way, we can all work together, enjoy the scenery, and have some friendly competition. Thanks for the suggestion!
assistant: A scavenger hunt is a perfect way to add an extra layer of excitement to your team hike! It's a great way to encourage teamwork, problem-solving, and communication while enjoying the beautiful scenery of Mount Tamalpais State Park.

Remember to create a list of items or challenges that are fun and achievable, and make sure to provide clear instructions and rules for the scavenger hunt. You could include items like:

* A specific type of leaf or flower
* A certain type of bird or insect
* A unique rock or mineral formation
* A scenic viewpoint or landmark
* A team photo in a specific location

You could also consider dividing the team into smaller groups to make it more competitive and exciting. Just make sure to set a time limit and a meeting point for everyone to gather and share their finds.

I'm sure your team will have an amazing time on the hike, and the scavenger hunt will add an extra layer of fun and engagement. Don't forget to take plenty of photos and share them with the team afterwards!

Have a fantastic time on your team hike, and I hope the scavenger hunt is a huge success!
